name: Phobos
notable_for: The larger and innermost of Mars's two moons; orbits so close and fast that it rises in the west and sets in the east; gradually spiraling inward and will eventually break apart into a ring

name: Deimos
notable_for: The smaller and outermost of Mars's two moons; the smallest known moon in the Solar System; named after the Greek god of dread

name: Io
notable_for: The most volcanically active body in the Solar System; tidal heating from Jupiter's gravity drives hundreds of active volcanoes; part of the Galilean moons

name: Europa
notable_for: Covered by a smooth icy shell concealing a vast subsurface liquid water ocean; one of the most promising candidates for extraterrestrial life in the Solar System

name: Ganymede
notable_for: The largest moon in the Solar System (larger than Mercury); the only moon known to have its own magnetic field; has a subsurface ocean

name: Callisto
notable_for: The most heavily cratered body in the Solar System; geologically dead and the oldest surface in the Solar System; the outermost of the Galilean moons

name: Amalthea
notable_for: The reddest object in the Solar System; radiates more heat than it receives from the Sun due to tidal heating and jovian magnetic field interactions

name: Himalia
notable_for: The largest of Jupiter's irregular outer moons; likely a captured Trojan asteroid; the brightest of Jupiter's irregular moons

name: Elara
notable_for: One of Jupiter's largest irregular moons; member of the Himalia group of prograde irregular satellites

name: Metis
notable_for: One of Jupiter's innermost moons; orbits within Jupiter's main ring and likely contributes material to it; inside Jupiter's Roche limit

name: Adrastea
notable_for: The smallest of the four inner Jovian moons; orbits at the outer edge of Jupiter's main ring and is considered the main source of ring material

name: Thebe
notable_for: Inner Jovian moon that gives rise to the diffuse Thebe ring (gossamer ring); heavily cratered surface with irregular shape

name: Leda
notable_for: One of Jupiter's small irregular prograde moons; member of the Himalia group; named after the mother of Castor, Pollux, Helen, and Clytemnestra in Greek mythology

name: Ananke
notable_for: The largest member of the Ananke group of retrograde irregular moons; has a retrograde orbit suggesting capture from the asteroid belt or Kuiper belt

name: Carme
notable_for: The largest member of the retrograde Carme group; likely a fragment of a captured Trojan asteroid; its group contains over 17 irregular moons

name: Sinope
notable_for: A retrograde irregular moon in the Pasiphae group; one of the most distant of Jupiter's known moons; named after a daughter of the river god Asopus

name: Pasiphae
notable_for: The largest of the retrograde Pasiphae group of Jovian irregular moons; named after the wife of King Minos in Greek mythology

name: Lysithea
notable_for: A prograde irregular moon in the Himalia group; named after one of Zeus's mortal lovers in Greek mythology

name: Aitne
notable_for: A small retrograde irregular moon belonging to the Ananke group; discovered as part of a large survey of outer Jovian moons

name: Ersa
notable_for: A small prograde irregular moon in the Himalia group; named after the daughter of Zeus and Selene (the goddess of dew)

name: Pandia
notable_for: A small prograde irregular moon in the Himalia group; named after the daughter of Zeus and Selene in Greek mythology

name: Carpo
notable_for: An isolated prograde irregular moon of Jupiter that does not belong to any of the established orbital groups; named after a daughter of Zeus and Themis

name: Titan
notable_for: The only moon in the Solar System with a dense atmosphere and stable bodies of liquid (methane and ethane lakes); second-largest moon in the Solar System; the Cassini-Huygens probe landed on its surface in 2005

name: Rhea
notable_for: Saturn's second-largest moon; thought to have a tenuous oxygen atmosphere; may have a faint ring system of its own — the first moon observed to potentially have rings

name: Iapetus
notable_for: Famous for its extreme two-toned coloring: one hemisphere is as dark as coal while the other is as bright as snow; has a prominent equatorial ridge

name: Dione
notable_for: Shows evidence of past geological activity with wispy terrain features; Cassini detected a tenuous oxygen atmosphere; may harbor a subsurface ocean

name: Tethys
notable_for: Features the enormous Odysseus crater (450 km wide) and the vast Ithaca Chasma canyon system stretching 2,000 km; nearly pure water ice composition

name: Enceladus
notable_for: Geologically active moon erupting water vapour and ice from its south pole in geysers; has a confirmed subsurface ocean; feeds material into Saturn's E ring; major target in the search for life

name: Mimas
notable_for: Famous for the Herschel crater (130 km wide) that gives it a resemblance to the Death Star; its resonance creates the Cassini Division in Saturn's rings

name: Hyperion
notable_for: The largest known irregularly shaped body in the Solar System; has a chaotic, tumbling rotation; its sponge-like surface has an unusually low density

name: Phoebe
notable_for: A captured Kuiper Belt object orbiting Saturn in a retrograde direction; the source of dark material coating Iapetus's leading hemisphere; the first moon discovered photographically

name: Janus
notable_for: Co-orbital moon that shares almost the same orbit as Epimetheus; every four years the two moons swap orbits in a gravitational dance instead of colliding

name: Epimetheus
notable_for: Co-orbital with Janus; the two moons exchange orbits every four years in the only known example of horseshoe co-orbital motion among moons

name: Helene
notable_for: A Trojan moon of Dione, sharing its orbit at the L4 Lagrangian point 60 degrees ahead of Dione; has an unusually smooth surface

name: Telesto
notable_for: A Trojan moon co-orbital with Tethys at the L4 point; named after a Oceanid in Greek mythology; has a smooth surface filled with regolith

name: Calypso
notable_for: A Trojan moon co-orbital with Tethys at the L5 point; named after the sea nymph in Homer's Odyssey; has a very smooth, rounded appearance

name: Atlas
notable_for: A shepherd moon that helps maintain the outer edge of Saturn's A ring; has a distinctive flying saucer shape due to material accumulation at its equator

name: Prometheus
notable_for: Inner shepherd moon of the F ring; gravitationally perturbs the F ring creating channels and kinks as it passes; elongated potato-like shape

name: Pandora
notable_for: Outer shepherd moon of the F ring; works together with Prometheus to confine the narrow F ring; irregular shape with many craters

name: Pan
notable_for: Orbits within the Encke Gap in Saturn's A ring, acting as a ring-clearing moon; has a prominent equatorial ridge giving it a distinctive walnut or ravioli shape

name: Miranda
notable_for: Has the most varied and extreme terrain of any Solar System body; features include Verona Rupes, a cliff 20 km high — the tallest known cliff in the Solar System; patchwork surface suggests geological disruption

name: Ariel
notable_for: The brightest and most recently active of Uranus's moons geologically; surface shows evidence of ancient flow deposits and tectonic rifting

name: Umbriel
notable_for: The darkest of the five major Uranian moons; uniformly dark and ancient surface with no signs of geological activity; mysterious bright ring feature in the Wunda crater

name: Titania
notable_for: The largest moon of Uranus; surface shows evidence of past geological activity including large fault systems (chasms); may harbor a subsurface ocean

name: Oberon
notable_for: The outermost of Uranus's five major moons; heavily cratered ancient surface with dark material of unknown origin on crater floors; named after the king of the fairies in Shakespeare's A Midsummer Night's Dream

name: Puck
notable_for: The largest of Uranus's inner moons discovered by Voyager 2; nearly spherical with a dark, cratered surface; named after the mischievous spirit in Shakespeare

name: Caliban
notable_for: The largest of Uranus's retrograde irregular moons; likely a captured body; named after the savage character in Shakespeare's The Tempest

name: Sycorax
notable_for: The second-largest irregular moon of Uranus; has a reddish hue possibly due to complex organic compounds; named after the witch mother of Caliban in Shakespeare's The Tempest

name: Triton
notable_for: The only large moon in the Solar System with a retrograde orbit; likely a captured Kuiper Belt object; geologically active with nitrogen geysers; spiraling inward and will eventually be torn apart into rings

name: Nereid
notable_for: Has one of the most eccentric orbits of any moon in the Solar System; its highly elliptical orbit suggests it was gravitationally perturbed when Triton was captured

name: Proteus
notable_for: The second-largest moon of Neptune; nearly as large as a sphere but irregular in shape; dark and heavily cratered; discovered by Voyager 2 due to its proximity to Neptune

name: Larissa
notable_for: One of Neptune's inner moons; discovered first by stellar occultation in 1981 and confirmed by Voyager 2 in 1989; irregularly shaped and heavily cratered

name: Charon
notable_for: So large relative to Pluto that the two are often considered a binary dwarf planet system; the two bodies are tidally locked to each other; surface shows red polar cap of tholins; its discovery was used to precisely calculate Pluto's mass

name: Nix
discoverer: Hubble Space Telescope (Hal Weaver et al.)
notable_for: One of the four small outer moons of Pluto; has a tumbling, chaotic rotation due to gravitational influence from Charon; named after the Greek goddess of night

name: Hydra
discoverer: Hubble Space Telescope (Hal Weaver et al.)
notable_for: The outermost and largest of Pluto's small moons; has a nearly white surface suggesting predominantly water ice; discovered along with Nix by the Hubble Space Telescope
